name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
حمٓ (١)
```

```english
Ha, Meem. (1)
```

```arabic
تَنزِيلٌ مِّنَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ (٢)
```

```english
A [gradually] sent down [revelation] from the All-beneficent, the All-merciful, (2)
```

```arabic
كِتَـٰبٌ فُصِّلَتْ ءَايَـٰتُهُۥ قُرْءَانًا عَرَبِيًّا لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ (٣)
```

```english
[this is] a Book whose signs have been elaborated for a people who have knowledge, an Arabic Quran, (3)
```

```arabic
بَشِيرًا وَنَذِيرًا فَأَعْرَضَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ فَهُمْ لَا يَسْمَعُونَ (٤)
```

```english
a bearer of good news and a warner. But most of them turn away [from it], [and] so they do not listen. (4)
```

```arabic
وَقَالُوا۟ قُلُوبُنَا فِىٓ أَكِنَّةٍ مِّمَّا تَدْعُونَآ إِلَيْهِ وَفِىٓ ءَاذَانِنَا وَقْرٌ وَمِنۢ بَيْنِنَا وَبَيْنِكَ حِجَابٌ فَٱعْمَلْ إِنَّنَا عَـٰمِلُونَ (٥)
```

```english
They say, ‘Our hearts are in veils [which shut them off] from what you invite us to, and there is a deafness in our ears, and there is a curtain between us and you. So act [as your faith requires]; we too are acting [according to our own].’ (5)
```

```arabic
قُلْ إِنَّمَآ أَنَا۠ بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُكُمْ يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَىَّ أَنَّمَآ إِلَـٰهُكُمْ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ فَٱسْتَقِيمُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ وَٱسْتَغْفِرُوهُ ۗ وَوَيْلٌ لِّلْمُشْرِكِينَ (٦)
```

```english
Say, ‘I am just a human like you. It has been revealed to me that your God is the One God. So worship Him single-mindedly and plead to Him for forgiveness.’ And woe to the polytheists (6)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْتُونَ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَهُم بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمْ كَـٰفِرُونَ (٧)
```

```english
—those who do not pay the zakat and disbelieve in the Hereafter. (7)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ لَهُمْ أَجْرٌ غَيْرُ مَمْنُونٍ (٨)
```

```english
As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, there will be an everlasting reward for them. (8)
```

```arabic
۞ قُلْ أَئِنَّكُمْ لَتَكْفُرُونَ بِٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلْأَرْضَ فِى يَوْمَيْنِ وَتَجْعَلُونَ لَهُۥٓ أَندَادًا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ رَبُّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٩)
```

```english
Say, ‘Do you really disbelieve in Him who created the earth in two days, and ascribe partners to Him? That is the Lord of all the worlds!’ (9)
```

```arabic
وَجَعَلَ فِيهَا رَوَٰسِىَ مِن فَوْقِهَا وَبَـٰرَكَ فِيهَا وَقَدَّرَ فِيهَآ أَقْوَٰتَهَا فِىٓ أَرْبَعَةِ أَيَّامٍ سَوَآءً لِّلسَّآئِلِينَ (١٠)
```

```english
He set in it firm mountains, [rising] above it, blessed it, and ordained in it, in four days, [various] means of sustenance, equally for all seekers. (10)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ ٱسْتَوَىٰٓ إِلَى ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَهِىَ دُخَانٌ فَقَالَ لَهَا وَلِلْأَرْضِ ٱئْتِيَا طَوْعًا أَوْ كَرْهًا قَالَتَآ أَتَيْنَا طَآئِعِينَ (١١)
```

```english
Then He turned to the heaven, and it was smoke, and He said to it and to the earth, ‘Come, willingly or unwillingly!’ They said, ‘We come obediently.’ (11)
```

```arabic
فَقَضَىٰهُنَّ سَبْعَ سَمَـٰوَاتٍ فِى يَوْمَيْنِ وَأَوْحَىٰ فِى كُلِّ سَمَآءٍ أَمْرَهَا ۚ وَزَيَّنَّا ٱلسَّمَآءَ ٱلدُّنْيَا بِمَصَـٰبِيحَ وَحِفْظًا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ تَقْدِيرُ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْعَلِيمِ (١٢)
```

```english
Then He set them up as seven heavens in two days, and revealed [to the angels] in each heaven its ordinance. We have adorned the lowest heaven with lamps, and guarded them. That is the ordaining of the All-mighty, the All-knowing. (12)
```

## (Commentary)

The Surah discusses their turning away from the Book revealed to them, which is the Noble Quran; this is its primary purpose. For this reason, you see the various strands of discourse revolving around it, beginning with it, and then returning to it section after section. It opens with His saying: «*A revelation from the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful*» (41:2) etc. Then it is said: «*The faithless say, ‘Do not listen to this Quran’*» (41:26) etc. And it is said: «*Indeed those who interpret Our signs profanely are not hidden from Us*» (41:40) etc. And it is said: «*Indeed those who defy the Reminder when it comes to them*» (41:41) etc. And it is said—at the conclusion of the discourse—: «*Say, ‘Tell me, if it is from Allah and then you disbelieve in it’*» (41:52) etc.

The necessary consequence of their turning away from the Book of Allah is the denial of the three principles (الأصول الثلاثة) that form the foundation of His true call: monotheism (الوحدانية), prophethood (النبوة), and the Resurrection (المعاد). Thus, the Surah expands the discourse on these matters, incorporating both glad tidings (التبشير) and warnings (الإنذار).

The Surah is Meccan (مكية), as the contents of its verses testify to that, and it is among the Surahs revealed in the early stages of the Prophetic mission (البعثة), according to what is gathered from the narrations.

His saying, the Exalted: «*Ha, Meem. A revelation from the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful*» (41:1-2) is the predicate (خبر) of a suppressed subject (مبتدأ), and the infinitive (المصدر) carries the meaning of the passive participle (المفعول). The estimation is: "This is a revelation (منزل) from the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful." The mention of these two noble attributes—the All-Beneficent (الرحمن), which indicates general mercy (الرحمة العامة) for both the believer and the disbeliever, and the Most Merciful (الرحيم), which indicates specific mercy (الرحمة الخاصة) for the believers—is to point out that this revelation rectifies the worldly life (دنياهم) of the people just as it rectifies their Hereafter (آخرتهم).

His saying, the Exalted: «*A Book whose signs have been elaborated, an Arabic Quran, for a people who have knowledge*» (41:3) is a predicate after a predicate. Elaboration (التفصيل) is the opposite of consolidation (الإحكام) and ambiguity (الإجمال). What is meant by the elaboration of the Quranic verses is the distinguishing of its parts from one another by bringing it down to the level of clear expression (البيان), such that the listener who is familiar with the styles of expression can understand its meanings and comprehend its objectives. To this points His saying, the Exalted: «*A Book whose signs have been made firm and then elaborated from One who is All-wise, All-aware*» (11:1), and His saying: «*By the Manifest Book! Indeed We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason. And indeed it is in the Mother of the Book with Us, surely exalted and wise*» (43:2-4).

His saying: «*an Arabic Quran*» (قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا) is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the "Book" or for its "signs." His saying: «*for a people who have knowledge*» (لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ)—the letter *lam* (اللام) is for causality (التعليل) or for specification (الاختصاص). The object (مفعول) of "they know" (يَعْلَمُونَ) is either suppressed—the estimation being: "for a people who know its meanings because they are familiar with the tongue in which it was revealed, namely the Arabs"—or it is omitted, meaning: "for a people who possess knowledge."

The implication of the first meaning is that there is a special regard for the Arabs in the Quran being revealed in Arabic. This is also suggested by His subsequent saying: «*Had We made it a non-Arabic Quran, they would have said, ‘Why have its signs not been elaborated? What! A non-Arabic [revelation] and an Arabian [prophet]?’*» (41:44). Similar to this is His saying: «*Had We sent it down to some of the non-Arabs, and had he recited it to them, they would not have believed in it*» (26:198-199).

This does not contradict the universality of his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) call to all of humanity, because his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) call was organized in stages. First, he called the people during the pilgrimage season (الموسم), where he was met with severe denial. Then he called secretly for a period. Then he was commanded to call his closest kin, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: «*And warn your nearest relations*» (26:214). Then he was commanded to call his people, as indicated by His saying: «*So proclaim what you have been commanded, and turn away from the polytheists*» (15:94). Then he was commanded to call people generally, as indicated by His saying: «*Say, ‘O mankind! I am indeed the Apostle of Allah to you all’*» (7:158), and His saying: «*This Quran has been revealed to me that I may warn you thereby and whomever it may reach*» (6:19).

Furthermore, it is historically established that among those who believed in him were Salman, who was Persian; Bilal, who was Abyssinian; and Suhayb, who was Roman. His call to the Jews and his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) encounters with them, as well as his letters to the kings of Persia, Egypt, Abyssinia, and Rome inviting them to Islam—all of this is evidence for the universality of the call.

His saying, the Exalted: «*as a bearer of good news and a warner. But most of them turn away, so they do not listen*» (41:4). "A bearer of good news and a warner" (بَشِيراً وَنَذِيراً) are two circumstantial qualifiers for the "Book" in the previous verse. The "listening" (السمع) that is negated here is the listening of acceptance (سمع القبول), as evidenced by the context of "turning away" (الإعراض).

His saying, the Exalted: «*They say, ‘Our hearts are in veils from what you invite us to’*» (41:5) until the end of the verse. Al-Raghib said: **The *kinn*** (الكن) is that in which a thing is preserved. He said: **The *kinan*** (الكنان) is the cover in which a thing is veiled, and the plural is *akinnah* (أكنة), like *ghita’* (غطاء) and *aghtiyah* (أغطية). The Exalted said: «*and We have cast veils on their hearts lest they should understand it*» (17:46). End quote.

So His saying: «*Our hearts are in veils from what you invite us to*» is a metaphor (كناية) for their hearts being such that they do not comprehend the monotheism (التوحيد) he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) invites them to, as if they are covered with veils that nothing from the outside can penetrate.

His saying: «*and there is a heaviness in our ears*» (وَفِي آذانِنا وَقْرٌ) means a weight of deafness, so they do not hear anything of this call. His saying: «*and there is a curtain between us and you*» (وَمِنْ بَيْنِنا وَبَيْنِكَ حِجابٌ) means a barrier that separates us from you, so we do not meet with you upon anything you desire. Thus, they made him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) despair of their accepting his call by informing him: first, that their hearts are in veils, so his call does not reach them such that they might comprehend it; second, that the pathways for it to reach the hearts—the ears—are blocked, so no call enters them and no warning or glad tidings penetrate them; and third, that between them and him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) a curtain has been drawn, so no common ground unites them with him. In this is the ultimate expression of despair.

His saying: «*So act! We [too] are acting*» (41:5) is a derivation (تفريع) from what preceded, and it is not devoid of a hint of threat. Accordingly, the meaning is: "Since there is no way for understanding between us, then do whatever you can to nullify our affair; indeed, we are acting to nullify your affair."

It has been said the meaning is: "Act according to your religion, for we are acting according to our religion." It has also been said: "Act toward our destruction, for we are acting toward your destruction." Both are somewhat far-fetched.

His saying, the Exalted: «*Say, ‘I am only a human like you. It has been revealed to me that your God is the One God. So be steadfast toward Him and plead to Him for forgiveness’*» (41:6) is in the position of answering their saying: «*Our hearts are in veils from what you invite us to*», as suggested by the context. Its summary is: "Say to them: I am only a human like you; I socialize with you as you socialize with one another, and I speak to you as one of you speaks to his companion. I am not of a species that differs from you, like an angel, such that there would be a curtain drawn between me and you, or that my speech would not penetrate your ears, or my words would not reach your hearts. However, that which I say to you and invite you toward is a revelation (وحي) revealed to me, and it is only that your God, who deserves to be worshipped, is One God, not disparate gods."

His saying: «*So be steadfast toward Him and plead to Him for forgiveness*» means: "Since there is no god but One God with no partner, then turn straight toward Him by affirming His oneness and negating partners from Him, and seek His forgiveness for the polytheism and sins that have issued from you."

His saying, the Exalted: «*Woe to the polytheists, those who do not pay the zakat and who are faithless in the Hereafter*» (41:6-7) is a threat to the polytheists (المشركين) who affirm partners for Allah and do not unify Him. He described them by two of their most specific characteristics: their failure to pay the *zakat* and their disbelief in the Hereafter.

What is meant by "paying the *zakat*" (إيتاء الزكاة) is the general spending of wealth on the poor and needy for the sake of Allah, for *zakat* in the sense of the obligatory alms in Islam had not yet been legislated when this Surah was revealed, as it is among the oldest Meccan Surahs.

It has been said that what is meant by "paying the *zakat*" is the purification of the soul (تزكية النفس) and its cleansing from the filth and impurity of sins, and its wholesome growth through the worship of Allah, the Glorified. This is a fine interpretation if the application of "paying the *zakat*" to such a meaning were linguistically sound.

His saying: «*and who are faithless in the Hereafter*» is another description of the polytheists, which is a necessary consequence of their creed—namely, the denial of the Resurrection (المعاد). For this reason, He used the separating pronoun (ضمير الفصل) to indicate that they are well-known for their disbelief in the Hereafter.

His saying, the Exalted: «*As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, there will be an everlasting reward for them*» (41:8). That is, a reward that is not cut off (غير مقطوع) but is continuous and eternal, as some have interpreted it. Others have interpreted it as "uncounted" (غير معدود), as the Exalted said: «*they shall be provided therein without any reckoning*» (40:40).

It is also permissible that what is meant is that there is no injury in it from the "reminding of favor" (المن) which sours a good deed. This view can be justified by the fact that naming what they are given as "reward" (الأجر) indicates this, as it suggests entitlement (الاستحقاق), even if this entitlement is by Allah's granting and not something they possess of themselves. The Exalted said: «*This is indeed your reward, and your endeavor has been appreciated*» (76:22).

His saying, the Exalted: «*Say, ‘Do you really disbelieve in Him who created the earth in two days, and set up equals to Him?’*» (41:9) etc. He commanded him a second time to question their disbelief in Allah—in the sense of their polytheism—despite the manifestation of the signs of His oneness in the creation of the heavens and the earth and the governance of their affairs, after He had first commanded him to rebut their saying: «*Our hearts are in veils*» etc.

The interrogation is for expressing wonder (التعجب); hence, the subject of the question was emphasized with *inna* (إن) and the letter *lam* (اللام), as if the questioner can hardly believe their disbelief in Allah and their assertion of equals (الأنداد) despite the clarity of the path and the soundness of the proof.

His saying: «*and set up equals to Him*» is an explanation of His saying: «*Do you really disbelieve in Him who created the earth*» etc. "Equals" (الأنداد) is the plural of *nidd* (ند), which means a peer or like. What is meant by "setting up equals to Him" is taking partners for Him who resemble Him in Lordship (الربوبية) and Divinity (الألوهية).

His saying: «*That is the Lord of all the worlds*» (ذلِكَ رَبُّ الْعالَمِينَ). In the use of the distal demonstrative (لفظ البعيد), there is an exaltation of His presence, the Exalted, and a sanctification of Him from such delusions. He is the Lord of all the worlds, the Governor of the affairs of all creation; thus, there is no justification for imagining another lord besides Him or another god other than Him.

What is meant by "day" (اليوم) in His saying: «*created the earth in two days*» is a period of time (برهة من الزمان), not the instance of the day we are familiar with while on the surface of this earth of ours—which is the duration of one rotation of the terrestrial globe around itself—for that is clearly invalid. The application of "day" to a segment of time that encompasses a particular event is very frequent and common in usage. Among such instances is His saying, the Exalted: «*and We alternate such days among mankind*» (3:140), and His saying: «*Do they expect anything but the like of the days of those who passed away before them?*» (10:102), and others.

So the "two days" in which Allah created the earth are two segments of time in which the formation of the earth as a complete earth was finished. In counting them as two days rather than one, there is evidence that the earth, during its initial period of formation, underwent two distinct stages, such as the stage of being raw and then ripe, or melting and then solidifying, or the like.

His saying, the Exalted: «*He set in it firm mountains [rising] above it*» (41:10) until the end of the verse. This is conjoined (معطوف) to His saying: «*created the earth in two days*». There is no harm in the two sentences: «*and set up equals to Him. That is the Lord of all the worlds*» intervening between the conjoined and the conjoined-to, because the first is an explanation of His saying: «*Do you really disbelieve*», and the second is a confirmation of the wonder expressed by the interrogation.

"Firm" (الرواسي) is an adjective for a suppressed noun; the estimation is: "firm mountains" (جبالا رواسي), meaning fixed upon the earth. The five feminine pronouns in the verse refer to the earth.

His saying: «*and blessed it*» (وَبارَكَ فِيها) means He placed in it much good (الخير الكثير) from which the plants, animals, and humans on the earth benefit in their lives through various types of utility.

His saying: «*and ordained in it its provisions in four days, equally for the seekers*» (41:10). It has been said: the adverbial phrase (الظرف), namely His saying: «*in four days*», involves a suppressed annexed noun (مضاف), and it is related to "ordained" (قدر). The estimation is: "He ordained the provisions in the completion of four days from the start of creation"—two days for the creation of the earth and two days—which complete the four days—for the ordaining of provisions.

It has also been said: it is related to the "attainment of provisions," with the suppressed annexed noun remaining as it is. The estimation is: "He ordained the attainment of its provisions in the completion of four days—in which He created the earth and its provisions altogether."

It has also been said: it is related to the attainment of all the mentioned matters: the setting of firm mountains above it, the blessing of it, and the ordaining of its provisions. The estimation is: "and the attainment of all that in the completion of four days." This involves much omission and estimation.

Al-Zamakhshari in *al-Kashshaf* made the adverbial phrase related to the predicate of a suppressed subject without estimating an annexed noun. The estimation is: "All that exists in four days." Thus, His saying: «*in four days*» is a kind of summary (فذلكة), as if it were said: "He created the earth in two days, and its provisions and other things in two days, so all of that is in four days."

They said: It was not permissible to interpret the verse as meaning that the setting of firm mountains and what followed it, or the ordaining of provisions, occurred in four days, because that would necessitate that the creation of the earth and what is in it took six days, and it is mentioned afterward that the heavens were created in two days, making the total eight days. Yet it is repeated in His speech, the Exalted, that He created the heavens and the earth in six days. This is the reason for interpreting the verse according to one of the previous views, despite the recourse to omission and estimation they involve.

In fairness, the verse—namely His saying: «*and ordained in it its provisions in four days, equally for the seekers*»—is apparent in a meaning other than what they mentioned. The surrounding context supports that what is meant is the ordaining of its provisions in the four seasons (الفصول الأربعة) produced by the northern and southern inclination of the sun according to sensory appearance. Thus, the "four days" are the four seasons.

The days of the creation of the heavens and the earth mentioned in these verses are four days: two days for the creation of the earth and two days for the leveling of the heavens into seven after they were smoke. As for the "days of provisions," they were mentioned as days for their *ordaining* (تقديرها), not for their *creation* (خلقها). What is repeated in His speech, the Exalted, is the *creation* of the heavens and the earth in six days, not the total of their creation and the ordaining of their affairs. The truth is that the adverbial phrase is a restriction for the last sentence only, and there is no omission or estimation in the verse. The intent is to explain the ordaining of the earth's provisions and sustenance in the four seasons of the year.

His saying: «*equally for the seekers*» (سَواءً لِلسَّائِلِينَ) is an absolute object (مفعول مطلق) for a suppressed verb, i.e., "the ordained provisions were equal (استوت) with an equality for the seekers." Or it is a circumstantial qualifier for the "provisions," i.e., "He ordained them in a state of being equal for the seekers," such that they all subsist on them and they suffice them without excess or deficiency.

The "seekers" (السائلون) are the various types of plants, animals, and humans, for they are in need of sustenance and provisions for their survival; thus, they are seekers of their Lord (1). The Exalted said: «*Everyone in the heavens and the earth asks Him*» (55:29), and He said: «*He gave you of all that you asked Him*» (14:34).

His saying, the Exalted: «*Then He turned to the heaven, and it was smoke, and He said to it and to the earth, ‘Come! willingly or unwillingly.’ They said, ‘We come willingly’*» (41:11).

**The turning** (الاستواء)—as mentioned by al-Raghib—when followed by the preposition *‘ala* (على), conveys the meaning of "dominion" (الاستيلاء), as in: «*The All-Beneficent, settled on the Throne*» (20:5). When followed by *ila* (إلى), it conveys the meaning of "reaching it" (الانتهاء إليه).

Also in *al-Mufradat*: **The *karh*** (الكره) with a *fathah* on the *kaf* is the hardship that reaches a person from the outside in what he is forced to do by compulsion; and **the *kurh*** (الكره) with a *dammah* on the *kaf* is what reaches him from his own self while he dislikes it.

So His saying: «*Then He turned to the heaven*» means He directed Himself toward it and intended it for creation, not a spatial intention (القصد المكاني) which cannot be achieved except by the intender moving from place to place and from direction to direction, for He, the Exalted, is transcendent above that.

The apparent meaning of the conjunction with *thumma* (ثم) is that the creation of the heavens was subsequent to the earth. However, it has been said that *thumma* is to indicate a delay in the report (التراخي بحسب الخبر), not in existence and realization. This is supported by His saying, the Exalted: «*Is it you who are more difficult to create or the heaven? He built it...*»—until He said—«*and after that He spread out the earth. He brought forth its water and its pasturage from it, and He set the mountains firm*» (79:27-32). This implies that the earth was subsequent to the heaven in creation.

The objection to this—that its implication is the delay of the "spreading out" (دحو) of the earth after the building of the heaven, and its spreading out is different from its creation—is refuted by the fact that the earth is spherical; thus, its spreading out and expansion is nothing other than its leveling as a sphere, which is its creation. Furthermore, after mentioning the spreading out of the earth, He pointed to the bringing forth of its water and pasturage and the setting firm of its mountains; these are exactly the "setting of firm mountains above it," "blessing it," and "ordaining its provisions" which He mentioned in the verses we are currently discussing along with the creation of the earth, and He conjoined the creation of the heaven to it with *thumma*. Thus, there is no choice but to interpret *thumma* as something other than temporal delay (التراخي الزماني), for His saying in the verse of *al-Nazi‘at*: «*after that*» (بَعْدَ ذلِكَ) is more explicit in temporal delay than the word *thumma* here in the verse of *Ha Meem al-Sajdah*. Allah knows best.

His saying: «*and it was smoke*» (وَهِيَ دُخانٌ) is a circumstantial qualifier for the "heaven," meaning He turned to the heaven for creation while it was a thing Allah named "smoke" (دخان). This was its matter (مادتها) which He clothed with form (الصورة) and decreed as seven heavens after they were not numbered or distinguished from one another. For this reason, He used the singular "heaven" (السماء) when He said: «*He turned to the heaven*».

His saying: «*and He said to it and to the earth, ‘Come! willingly or unwillingly’*» is a derivation (تفريع) from His turning to the heaven. The context is undoubtedly one of "bringing into being" (التكوين). Thus, His saying to it and to the earth: «*Come! willingly or unwillingly*» is a word of origination (كلمة إيجاد) and a creative command (أمر تكويني), like His saying to a thing whose existence He intends: "Be!" The Exalted said: «*All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it, ‘Be,’ and it is*» (36:82).

The totality of His saying to them: «*Come!*» etc., and their saying to Him: «*We come*» etc., is a representation (تمثيل) of the attribute of origination and creation according to simple conventional understanding, and an analytical reality (حقيقة تحليلية) based on what is gathered from His speech, the Exalted, regarding the pervasiveness of knowledge (سراية العلم) in all existents and that the "speaking" to every thing is according to what suits its state. We have presented some discussion on this in previous sections, and a portion of the discussion will follow in the commentary on His saying: «*They will say, ‘Allah gave us speech, [He] who gives speech to all things’*» (41:21) in this Surah, if Allah wills.

The saying of some—that what is meant by His saying: «*Come!*» etc., is His commanding them to manifest the effects and benefits within them, rather than a command to be originated and created—is refuted by the fact that the "becoming" (تكون) of the heaven is mentioned afterward, and there is no meaning in preceding the command to manifest effects and benefits before mentioning the "becoming."

In His saying: «*Come! willingly or unwillingly*», there is the making of "coming" obligatory upon them and giving them the choice between doing so willingly or unwillingly. Perhaps what is meant by "willingly" and "unwillingly"—which are, in a sense, the acceptance of the act and a type of compatibility or its absence—is the prior aptitude (الاستعداد السابق) for existence or its absence. Thus, His saying: «*Come! willingly or unwillingly*» is a metaphor for the necessity of their coming without fail, and that it is a command that never fails to be realized, whether they willed it or disliked it, whether they asked for it or did not. So they answered that they comply with the command out of prior aptitude, essential acceptance, and primordial request (سؤال فطري), as they said: «*We come willingly*» (أَتَيْنا طائِعِينَ).

The saying of some—that His saying: «*willingly or unwillingly*» is a representation of the inevitability of the effect of His power, the Exalted, upon them and the impossibility of their refusing that, rather than an affirmation of "will" and "dislike" for them—is refuted by His subsequent saying: «*They said, ‘We come willingly’*». For if the mentioned alternative were only a representation without affirmation, as he mentioned, there would be no reason for affirming "willingness" in the response.

His saying: «*They said, ‘We come willingly’*» is the response of the heaven and the earth to His address, the Exalted, by choosing willingness. The expression using the term specific to rational beings (أولي العقل)—"willingly" (طائِعِينَ) [in the masculine plural]—is due to the context of address and response, which are characteristics of rational beings. The expression using the plural form [rather than the dual "the two of us come willingly"] is perhaps out of humility on their part, considering themselves as not distinct from the rest of His creatures who are obedient to His command; thus, they answered on behalf of all. This is similar to what has been said regarding His saying, the Exalted: «*You [alone] do we worship and to You [alone] do we look for help*» (1:5).

Furthermore, the joining of the earth with the heaven in the address «*Come!*» etc., despite the mention of its creation and the governance of its affairs previously, is not devoid of a hint that there is a type of connection in existence and a continuity in the system (النظام) running through them. This is indeed the case, for action and passion (الفعل والانفعال), and influence and being influenced (التأثير والتأثر), circulate among the parts of the witnessed world.

In His saying: «*and He said to it and to the earth*», there is an allusion, in any case, to the fact that *thumma* in His saying: «*Then He turned*» is for delay according to the rank of the discourse (رتبة الكلام).

His saying, the Exalted: «*Then He completed them as seven heavens in two days and revealed to each heaven its order*» (41:12). The root meaning of *qada’* (القضاء) is the deciding of a matter. The pronoun in "He completed them" (فَقَضاهُنَّ) refers to the "heaven" in meaning. "Seven heavens" is a circumstantial qualifier for the pronoun, and "in two days" is related to "He completed them." Thus, the sentence conveys that when He, the Glorified, turned to the heaven while it was smoke, its affair was ambiguous and unspecified in terms of the actuality of existence; so He, the Exalted, decided its affair by making it seven heavens in two days.

It has been said that "completion" (القضاء) in the verse incorporates the meaning of "transforming" (التصيير), and "seven heavens" is its second object. Other views have been mentioned which we need not present.

The verse and what preceded it look toward the elaboration of what was summarized in His saying: «*Have the faithless not regarded that the heavens and the earth were a solid mass and We clove them asunder?*» (21:30).

His saying: «*and revealed to each heaven its order*» (وَأَوْحى فِي كُلِّ سَماءٍ أَمْرَها). It has been said: what is meant by the "order of the heaven" (أمر السماء) is that for which it is prepared or what wisdom necessitates within it, such as the existence of an angel or a star and the like. "Revelation" (الوحي) here is creation and origination (الخلق والإيجاد). The sentence is conjoined to His saying: «*He completed them*», restricted by the time mentioned for the conjoined-to. The meaning is: "and He created in each heaven what is in it of angels, stars, and others."

You are well aware that intending "creation" from "revelation," and things like "angels and stars" from "order," requires an extra interpretive effort (عناية زائدة) that is not established except by clear evidence. Likewise, the restriction of the conjoined sentence by the time mentioned in the conjoined-to.

It has also been said: what is meant by "order" is the divine obligation (التكليف الإلهي) directed toward the inhabitants of each heaven among the angels, and "revelation" carries its well-known meaning. The meaning is: "and He revealed to the inhabitants of each heaven among the angels what He commanded them of worship."

Against this is that the apparent meaning of the verse—where He, the Exalted, said: «*in each heaven*» (فِي كُلِّ سَماءٍ) and did not say: "to each heaven" (إلى كل سماء)—does not perfectly align with this.

It has also been said: what is meant by "its order" is what Allah willed from it. This view, in reality, returns to one of the two previous views: if "revelation" is intended as creation and origination, it returns to the first; if it is intended in its well-known meaning, it returns to the second.

That which has occurred in His speech, the Exalted, regarding the "order" (الأمر) related in some way to the heaven, alludes to a meaning more subtle than what they mentioned. For He, the Exalted, said: «*He directs the command from the heaven to the earth; then it ascends to Him*» (32:5), and He said: «*It is Allah who has created seven heavens, and of the earth [a number] similar to them. The command gradually descends through them*» (65:12), and He said: «*Certainly We have created seven ways above you, and We have not been oblivious of the creation*» (23:17).

The first verse indicates that the heaven is the source for His command, the Exalted, which descends to the earth in some way. The second indicates that the command descends through the heavens from heaven to heaven until it reaches the earth. The third indicates that the heavens are "ways" (طرائق) for the passage of the command from the Possessor of the Throne, or for the passage of the angels who carry the command to the earth, as indicated by His saying: «*In it the angels and the Spirit descend, by the leave of their Lord, with every command*» (97:4), and His saying: «*In it every definitive command is dispatched*» (44:4).

If what is meant by "the command" (الأمر) is His creative command (أمره التكويني)—which is the word of origination (كلمة الإيجاد), as gathered from His saying: «*All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it, ‘Be’*» (36:82)—then the verses, by joining some to others, would imply that the divine command whose passage in the terrestrial world is the creation of things and the occurrence of events, is carried by the angels from the presence of Him, the Exalted, the Possessor of the Throne. They traverse the ways of the heavens in its descent, bringing it down from heaven to heaven until they reach the earth with it.

The angels of each heaven only carry it to those below them, as gathered from His saying: «*until when fear is lifted from their hearts, they say, ‘What did your Lord say?’ They say, ‘The truth, and He is the All-exalted, the All-great’*» (34:23); we have already discussed this. The heavens are the dwellings of the angels, as gathered from His saying: «*How many an angel there is in the heavens*» (53:26), and His saying: «*They cannot listen to the Exalted Assembly and are pelted from every side*» (37:8).

Thus, the command has a relation to each heaven by virtue of the angels dwelling in it, and a relation to each group of angels carrying it by virtue of its being entrusted to them; this is His "revealing" (وحيه) it to them, for Allah, the Glorified, named it "speech" (قولا), as He said: «*Our word to a thing, when We will it, is but to say to it, ‘Be’*» (16:40).

It follows from what has passed that the meaning of His saying: «*and revealed to each heaven its order*» is: He revealed in each heaven to its inhabitants among the angels the divine command attributed to that heaven and related to it. As for the "two days" mentioned in the verse being the time-frame for this revelation, just as they are the time-frame for the creation of the heavens as seven, there is no evidence for this in the wording of the verse.

His saying, the Exalted: «*and We adorned the lowest heaven with lamps, and for protection. That is the ordaining of the All-mighty, the All-knowing*» (41:12). Describing this heaven as "the lowest" (الدنيا) is to indicate that it is the closest of the heavens to the earth, and they are layers (طباق) one above the other, as He said: «*created seven heavens in layers*» (67:3).

The apparent meaning of its being adorned with "lamps" (مصابيح)—which are the stars, as He said: «*Indeed We have adorned the lowest heaven with the adornment of the stars*» (37:6)—is that the stars are in the lowest heaven or below it, like suspended lanterns. If they were scattered throughout all the heavens without some veiling others—due to the heavens being transparent, as has been said—they would be an adornment for all of them, and the adornment would not be specific to some, as the context implies. Thus, there is no basis for the one who says: "They are in all of them, but because they are seen twinkling upon the lowest heaven, they were counted as an adornment for it."

As for His saying: «*Have you not regarded how Allah created the seven heavens in layers, and made the moon a light in them, and made the sun a lamp?*» (71:15-16), this is in relation to us, the assembly of those who seek light by night and day, like His saying: «*and We made a radiant lamp*» (78:13).

His saying: «*and for protection*» (وَحِفْظاً) means: "and We protected it from the devils with a protection," as He said: «*and We have protected them from every outcast devil, except him who steals a hearing, whereat a manifest flame pursues him*» (15:17-18).

His saying: «*That is the ordaining of the All-mighty, the All-knowing*» is an allusion to the aforementioned order and arrangement.

## (A Discourse for Completion)

From what has preceded, it is gathered:

First: What is gathered from the apparent meaning of the noble verses—though they are not definitive (ليست بنص)—is that the "lowest heaven" among these seven is the world of stars and planets above us.

Second: These seven mentioned heavens are all of the corporeal creation (الخلق الجسماني); they are like seven layers of the world of bodies (عالم الأجسام) corresponding to one another, the closest of which to us is the world of stars and planets. The Quran did not describe any of the remaining six heavens except to mention that they are in layers (طباق).

Third: What is meant by the "seven heavens" is not the celestial bodies (الأجرام العلوية) or specifically some of them like the sun and the moon or others.

Fourth: What has been narrated regarding the heavens being dwellings for the angels, and that they descend from them by the command of Allah carrying it and ascend to them with the records of deeds, and that the heaven has gates that are not opened for the disbelievers, and that things and provisions descend from it, and other such matters pointed to by various verses and narrations—all of this reveals that these matters have a type of connection to these heavens. This connection is not like the connection of the bodies we see to their corporeal locations and places, which necessitates the governance of the material system (النظام المادي) over them and the infiltration of change, transformation, decay, and languor into them.

This is because it is certain today that these celestial bodies—whatever they may be—have an elemental, corporeal existence (كينونة عنصرية جسمانية) in which judgments and effects similar to those in our elemental terrestrial world occur. The system established for the heaven, its inhabitants, and the matters occurring within it—to which we have alluded—is distinct from this witnessed elemental system. Add to that what has been narrated: that the angels were created from light, that their food is glorification (التسبيح), the descriptions of their creation, the descriptions of the creation of the heavens and what was created in them, and so on.

Thus, for the angels, there are seven celestial realms (عوالم ملكوتية) arranged in order, named "seven heavens." Their specific properties and effects were attributed to the outward appearance of these heavens in consideration of their height and encompassment in relation to the earth, to facilitate simple understanding.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Ibn Abi Shaybah, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, Abu Ya‘la, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), Ibn Mardawayh, Abu Nu‘aym, and al-Bayhaqi (both in *al-Dala’il*), and Ibn ‘Asakir narrated from Jabir ibn ‘Abdillah, who said: "The Quraysh gathered one day and said: 'Look for the most knowledgeable among you in sorcery, soothsaying, and poetry—let him go to this man who has divided our community, scattered our affair, and criticized our religion—let him speak to him and see what he returns with.' They said: 'We know of no one other than ‘Utbah ibn Rabi‘ah.' They said: 'You, O Abu al-Walid.'"

So he went to him and said: "O Muhammad, are you better or ‘Abdullah? Are you better or ‘Abd al-Muttalib?" The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) remained silent. He said: "If you claim that these are better than you—they worshipped the gods you have criticized—and if you claim you are better than them—then speak so we may hear from you."

"By Allah, we have never seen a person more ill-omened (أشأم) upon your people than you—you have divided our community, scattered our affair, criticized our religion, and disgraced us among the Arabs—until it has spread among them that among the Quraysh is a sorcerer, and among the Quraysh is a soothsayer. By Allah, we wait for nothing but a cry like that of a pregnant woman—that some of us should rise against others with swords. O man, if it is need that has driven you, we will gather for you—until you are the wealthiest man of the Quraysh. And if it is desire for women that has driven you, then choose whichever women of the Quraysh you wish—we will marry you to ten."

The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "Have you finished?" He said: "Yes." Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: «*In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful. Ha, Meem. A revelation from the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful. A Book whose signs have been elaborated, an Arabic Quran, for a people who have knowledge*» until he reached «*But if they turn away, then say, ‘I warn you of a thunderbolt, like the thunderbolt of ‘Ad and Thamud’*».

‘Utbah said: "Enough! Do you have anything other than this?" He said: "No." So he returned to the Quraysh, and they said: "What is behind you?" He said: "I did not leave anything I thought you would say to him except that I said it." They said: "Did he answer you?" He said: "By the One who erected it as a structure, I did not understand anything of what he said—except that he said: ‘I warn you of a thunderbolt like the thunderbolt of ‘Ad and Thamud.’" They said: "Woe to you! The man speaks to you in Arabic and you do not know what he said?" He said: "No, by Allah, I did not understand anything of what he said except the mention of the thunderbolt."

I say: It was narrated from several books in a similar fashion. In some chains: "They said: 'What is behind you, O Abu al-Walid?' He said: 'By Allah, I have heard a speech the like of which I have never heard. By Allah, it is not poetry, nor sorcery, nor soothsaying. By Allah, the speech I heard shall surely have a great consequence (نبأ).'" In some, it is other than that.

Regarding his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) recitation of the verses from the beginning of the Surah to al-Walid ibn al-Mughirah, there is another narration that will reach you, if Allah wills, in the commentary of Surah al-Muddaththir, under His saying, the Exalted: «*Leave Me to [deal with] him whom I created alone*» (74:11) etc.

In the same source: Ibn Jarir narrated from Abu Bakr, who said: "The Jews came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: 'O Muhammad—inform us what Allah created of the creation in these six days?' He said: 'Allah created the earth on Sunday and Monday, and He created the mountains on Tuesday, and He created the cities, provisions, and rivers—their cultivation and their ruin—on Wednesday, and He created the heavens and the angels on Thursday—until three hours (meaning of Friday), and He created in the first hour the lifespans (الآجال), in the second the blight (الآفة), and in the third Adam.' They said: 'You have spoken the truth, if you complete it.'" The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) recognized what they intended and became angry, so Allah revealed: «*and no fatigue touched Us. So be patient with what they say*» (50:38-39).

I say: Something close to this was narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas, ‘Abdullah ibn Salam, ‘Ikrimah, and others. It has also appeared in some Shi‘i reports. Their saying: "You have spoken the truth, if you complete it" means: "if you complete your speech regarding creation by saying that He, the Exalted, finished creation on Saturday and rested on it."

The narrations are not devoid of issues:

Firstly: Because they include the Jews' confirmation of what was mentioned regarding the order of creation, which explicitly contradicts what is in the first chapter of Genesis in the Torah. In it, He created light and darkness—day and night—on Sunday; He created the heaven on Monday; He created the earth, seas, and plants on Tuesday; He created the sun, moon, and stars on Wednesday; He created the creatures of the sea and birds on Thursday; and He created the land animals and man on Friday, finishing creation on Saturday and resting on it. The claim that the present Torah is different from what was in the time of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is as you see.

Secondly: Because a "day" of the week—which is a daytime with its night—depends for its existence on the rotational movement (الحركة الوضعية) of the earth once before the sun. So what is the meaning of creating the earth in two days when the heaven and celestial bodies had not yet been created, nor had the earth been completed as a moving sphere? A similar problem applies to the creation of the heaven and celestial bodies, including the sun, when there is no "day" where there is no sun yet.

Thirdly: Because it counted a day for the creation of mountains, yet scientific investigation has concluded that they are created gradually. A similar problem applies to the creation of cities, rivers, and provisions.

In *Rawdat al-Kafi*, with its chain to Muhammad ibn ‘Atiyyah from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), that he said: "**And He created the thing from which all things are—and it is the water from which He created all things—so He made the lineage of every thing return to water—and He did not make for water a lineage to which it is attributed. And He created the wind from the water.**"

"**Then He gave the wind power over the water, so the wind split the surface of the water—until foam rose from the water as much as He willed it to rise—so He created from that foam a white, pure earth—in which there was no crack or hole—nor ascent or descent, nor tree—then He folded it and placed it above the water.**"

"**Then Allah created fire from the water, so the fire split the surface of the water—until smoke rose from the water as much as Allah willed it to rise—so He created from that smoke a clear, pure heaven—in which there was no crack or hole—and that is His saying: ‘the heaven, He built it.’**"

I say: There are some other narrations with this meaning. It is possible to apply what is in the narration, as well as the contents of the verses, to what has been accepted by scientific research today regarding the creation of the world and its form. However, we have refrained from that to avoid limiting Quranic truths by scientific conjectures and hypotheses, as long as a hypothesis is not definitively proven through scientific demonstration (البرهان العلمي).

In *Nahj al-Balaghah*: "**Among the witnesses of His creation is the creation of the heavens—established without pillars, standing without support. He called them and they answered, obedient and submissive—without hesitation or delay. Had it not been for their acknowledgment of His Lordship and their submission to Him in obedience—He would not have made them a place for His Throne, nor a dwelling for His angels—nor a place of ascent for the goodly word and righteous deed from His creation.**"

In *Kamal al-Din*, with its chain to Fudayl al-Rassan, who said: "Muhammad ibn Ibrahim wrote to Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): 'Inform us of your merit, O People of the House (أهل البيت)?' So Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) wrote to him: '**Indeed the stars were made as a security for the inhabitants of the heaven—so when the stars of the heaven go, there comes to the inhabitants of the heaven what they were promised. And the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: My People of the House were made as a security for my community—so when my People of the House go, there comes to my community what they were promised.**'"

I say: This meaning has appeared in more than one narration.

In *al-Bihar*, from the book *al-Gharat*, with its chain to Ibn Nubatah, who said: "Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) was asked: 'How much is between the heaven and the earth?' He said: '**The reach of the sight and the prayer of the oppressed.**'"

I say: This is among the subtleties of his (peace be upon him) speech, alluding to the outward aspect of the heaven and its inward aspect, as previously discussed.
